title: the European Parliament adopts new law to fight global deforestation
bodyHtml:
The regulation against deforestation has been approved by the European Parliament.

The Deforestation Regulation will affect companies that sell certain commodities to EU countries or that export to EU-based companies.

The regulation does not ban any country or product outright, but companies will be able to sell their products in the European Union if they provide a "due diligence declaration" that the product does not contribute to deforestation or forest degradation.

Under the declaration, companies will have to confirm that their products comply with relevant legislation and that indigenous peoples have not been harmed during the production process.

The new regulation covers beef, cocoa, coffee, rubber, palm oil, soy, and timber products, as well as other products that contain or are derived from these items. For example, wooden packagings such as crates and boxes, tableware, kitchenware and furniture, items and clothing accessories made from vulcanized and hardened rubber, printed books, newspapers, pictures, and brochures will be subject to the regulation.

After the regulation comes into force, the Commission will classify countries or regions as low-risk, standard, or high-risk 18 months later. Products from low-risk countries will be subject to a simplified due diligence procedure. Producers exporting goods from high-risk areas will face a different due diligence procedure.

Penalties for non-compliant companies will be significant and maximum fines will be set at least 4 percent of the company's total annual turnover in the EU.

The Deforestation Regulation will be effective 18 months after the obligations set out in the regulation are published in the EU Official Journal, which will be 20 days after the regulation is published.
